does: This paper introduces Cultural Awareness Reward modeling Benchmark (CARB), a new evaluation dataset spanning 10 cultures across 4 cultural domains, to assess how well reward models capture cultural nuances. The authors evaluate state-of-the-art reward models on CARB and demonstrate that their performance correlates with downstream multilingual cultural alignment tasks. Through detailed analysis, they uncover that existing RMs rely on spurious surface-level correlations rather than genuine cultural understanding. To remedy this, the paper proposes Think-as-Locals, a method that uses reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards (RLVR) to encourage generative reward models to engage in deeper culturally grounded reasoning that properly reflects diverse cultural values and preferences.
connects: This work builds on the growing recognition that LLM alignment requires cultural sensitivity beyond monolithic preference modeling. It extends prior work on reward model evaluation, which traditionally focused on helpfulness and harmlessness metrics without cultural specificity. The paper responds to limitations in existing multilingual and cross-cultural NLP evaluation benchmarks by creating a culture-centered benchmark specifically for reward models. It engages with recent research on reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) and reward learning, while also connecting to broader efforts in fair and inclusive AI that address representation of underrepresented cultures in training data and evaluation frameworks.
enables: This work establishes foundation and tooling for developing reward models that genuinely respect cultural diversity in global LLM alignment. The CARB benchmark provides a reusable evaluation framework that future work can build upon to assess cultural awareness improvements. The Think-as-Locals approach demonstrates a path toward more culturally grounded reward modeling through targeted reinforcement learning, which could inspire similar techniques for other value-laden domains. This research opens opportunities for developing methods that reduce spurious correlations in reward models more broadly, and points toward deeper investigation of how to embed authentic cultural knowledge rather than surface patterns into preference learning systems for truly inclusive AI alignment.
